Oracc: CAMS/GKAB — CAMS Geography of Knowledge Corpus

STT 2, 360 [Elegies Mourning the Death of Tammuz][via cams/gkab]

Obverse
o 1o 1

[mu-pi-šu]-u lu₂DAM.GAR₃u ⸢KA₂⸣.DINGIR-a-a

(o 1) The dealer, the Babylonian merchant, [who] gave (his) word, has destroyed his donkeys. The dog was killed together with our flock.

o 22

[ša] ⸢KA⸣ SUM⸢ni?⸣ u₂-sa-ri-⸢ḫi⸣-a ANŠE.NITA₂-MEŠ-šu₂

o 33

⸢UR⸣.GI₇u₂ GAZ⸢ku⸣ qa-⸢ni⸣ [ṣe]-ni-ni1

o 44

mu-pi-šu-u lu₂⸢DAM⸣.[GAR₃u KA₂].DINGIR-a-a

(o 4) The dealer, the Babylonian merchant, [who] gave (his) word, has destroyed his donkeys. The dog was killed together with our flock.

o 55

ša KA SUMni ⸢u₂⸣-[sa-ri]-⸢ḫi⸣-a ANŠE.NITA₂-<MEŠ>-šu₂

o 66

UR.GI₇u₂ i-du-⸢ku⸣ [qa]-⸢ni⸣ ṣe-ni-ni

o 77

ŠEŠ ⸢ŠA₃? UŠ⸣ x x [...] ⸢x KI⸣ du-gul

(o 7) The brother, ... [...] ... look!

o 88

[du]-gul i-mu-x [...] x-te-e DUMU.MUNUSni

(o 8) Look ... [...] ... our daughter.

o 99

[...] KAL MU x [...] ⸢e-ti⸣-iq

(o 9) [...] ... [...] proceed!

o 1010

⸢ik⸣-kil ⸢nu⸣-[... a]-⸢na⸣ e-rab ba-a-⸢di⸣

(o 10) We [...] a lamentation at the onset of the evening, in accordance with what [...] ... us, like [...] ... [...] ... our daughter.

o 1111

⸢ki⸣-i ⸢ša⸣ [...] x-ta-ni-ni

o 1212

ki-i [...] x x [...]-⸢te⸣-e DUMU.MUNUSni

o 1313

E₂ ⸢AD-ia AD?⸣ [...] IGI ŠE e-tap-aš₂

(o 13) I have made1 my father's house ... [...] ... like the temple of the god of dawn. I have heard [...] ....

o 1414

⸢a-ki-i⸣ [E₂] DINGIR na-⸢ma⸣-[ri ...] x-ma-ti-šu₂ a-sa-me

o 1515

E₂ AD ma-⸢ṣi ina⸣ U₄ 10 a-na ⸢E₂ DINGIR⸣ ba-di ut-te-ru-šu₂

(o 15) The paternal house - already on the 10th day, they have turned it into a temple of the evening god. The have done (this) to it, (and) it ... [...] like the temple of the god of dawn.

o 1616

a-na ša₂-šu₂ e-tap-šu šu-u ki-i ⸢E₂⸣ DINGIR na-ma-⸢ri⸣ i-ti-[...]

o 1717

lu₂SIPA ina UGU ṣe-⸢ni⸣ GAZ lu₂ENGAR ina UGU gišAPIN

(o 17) The shepherd was killed on top of the flock, the farmer on top of the plough; the gardener, he was killed in the orchard; the šandabakku2 was killed on his fields.

o 1818

lu₂NU.gišKIRI₆ ina gišKIRI₆ GAZ šu-u-tu₂

o 1919

lu₂GU₂.EN.NA GAZ ina UGU ma-na-⸢ḫa-te⸣-šu₂

o 2020

bak-ka-ni-i ⸢ṣar-piš⸣ ana lu₂NU.gišKIRI₆ni ni-ib-ki

(o 20) We are crying loud and bitterly. We weep for our gardener, for our gardener, for our šandabakku, whose fruit we wholeheartedly ate, (who) was praised in bunches of grapes (and) in wine.

o 2121

⸢ana⸣ lu₂NU.gišKIRI₆⸢ni⸣-i <<x>> ana lu₂GU₂.⸢EN⸣.NAni

o 2222

⸢ša₂⸣ a-ni-nu ina ⸢gu-um⸣ lib₃-bi-⸢ni⸣ ni-ku-⸢la⸣-a i-⸢nab⸣-šu₂

o 2323

ina ŠA₃ gišKIN.GEŠTIN-MEŠ ina giš⸢GEŠTIN u₂-ša₂⸣-ad-lil-ni

o 2424

⸢IRI⸣.ŠE-ki a-ḫa-ti ⸢nu-ku⸣ la-li-⸢ka⸣

(o 24) "(To) your village, my sister," I said, "I want to go, but where is it? They have returned (me) alone to your feet."

o 2525

a-a-ka-ma ina GIR₃-MIN-ki-i ⸢e⸣-du ut-te-ru <<BU>> [(...)]

o 2626

e-ru-bu gu-zi?-ru [ina] ⸢tur⸣-te-e ŠU.SI-ki [(...)]

(o 26) They entered the guzīru [at] the bending of your finger.

o 2727

DUMU-ia ina GIR₃-MIN-⸢ia⸣-a e-du ut-te-⸢ru⸣-[ka]

(o 27) My son, they have returned [you] alone to my feet.

o 2828

[...] ⸢AD⸣-ma ši-[i]-bi-ia ⸢te⸣-x x [...]

(o 28) [...] ... my witnesses ... [...]

o 2929

[...] ⸢DU⸣ UD ⸢RA⸣ [...] ⸢I⸣ ZA NIN a-⸢na?⸣ [...]

(o 29) [...] ... [...] ... to [...]

o 3030

[...] x-ma-a-ni x NIM x [...]

(o 30) [...] ... [...].

r 1r 1

[...]

(r 1) [...]

r 22

ka-[...]

(r 2) ... [...].

r 33

⸢a⸣-ni-na [...]

(r 3) Hear me [...]!

r 44

GEME₂ NIN [...]

(r 4) The sister's maid [...].

r 55

iṣ-ṣur-tu₂ ša uš-x [...] x x x ⸢GAB?⸣ BA TA x [...] x

(r 5) The female bird which ... [...] ... [...] ....

r 66

a-da-ni-ka A MAN ap-pa-⸢re⸣-e gišTIR i-na-[ki]-is

(r 6) Attention, (crown) prince! He cuts down the reed-beds (and) forest. He made the female birds that are in them flee (so that) they made nests within your city.

r 77

MUŠEN-MEŠte ša i-si-i-ši-⸢na u₂⸣-ša₂-bit-⸢ni?⸣

r 88

am-ma-⸢te⸣ ina bir-ti IRI-⸢ki? qin-ni⸣ iq-ṭu-na-ni

r 99

⸢ia-um-ma⸣ ina ⸢UB?⸣ x x [...] x i-ša₂-bar AD KI.MIN

(r 9) (My) father sends mine in ... [...] .... Ditto.

r 1010

[...] ⸢DI?⸣ I ⸢ḪU? RA?⸣ x [...] ⸢AN?⸣ x x-e la le-ʾu

(r 10) [...] ... [...] unable

r 1111

[...] x x x ME LU [...] e-pu-ša₂ E₂

(r 11) [...] ... [...] built a house

r 1212

⸢ki⸣-i x x x NE e-pu-[...] IZI u₂-sa-me-ka-ni

(r 12) like ... [...], he smothered the fire for me.

r 1313

ia-um-ma ki-i ḫa-⸢an⸣-[ni-e] x TI? ⸢i?-pu⸣-ša₂ E₂

(r 13) He built my house like this ...

r 1414

a-na-ku ina gišKIRI₆-ni-i ki-i [GIŠ SIG₅] e-la-ku

(r 14) In our orchard I stood tall like [a fine tree]. I stood tall like a lammu-tree; I stood tall like a pine-tree. Like a fine tree, I stood tall in the gate of processions.

r 1515

e-la-⸢ku⸣ ki lam-me-e ⸢e-la-ku⸣ [ki]-⸢i⸣ a-⸢šu⸣-ḫi-ma

r 1616

a-ki GIŠ SIG₅ e-la-ku ina KA₂ ma-⸢la⸣-ka-a-ni

r 1717

⸢e-mu⸣-ra-ni i-ḫa-di-⸢i⸣ AD-u-a ḫa-de-eš

(r 17) My father saw me (and) rejoiced greatly. Where is it? You looked at me (and) you rejoiced like ... .

r 1818

⸢a-a-ka?-a?⸣ tap-pal-sa-a-⸢ni⸣ ki-i x x-e ta-ri-[iš]

r 1919

u₂-ma-⸢a i⸣-tak-su-ni ina? ⸢mal?⸣-di-⸢ri⸣ u₂-se-bi-lu-u-⸢ni⸣

(r 19) Now they have cut me down, they have sent me off forever.

r 2020

e-⸢mu-ra⸣-[ni]-i i-bak-ki-i AD-u-a KI.MIN<(e-mu-ra-ni-i)> i-bak-ki-i AD-u-a

(r 20) My father saw me (and) wept. Ditto (my father saw me and wept).

r 2121

a-a-[ka?-a tap]-⸢pal⸣-sa-a-ni ⸢ta?⸣-ḫa-di-[i] ⸢mi⸣-tu₂

(r 21) Where is it? You looked at me (and) became joyful, O dead one.

r 2222

ša an-ni-⸢u⸣ ta-ki-sa me-nu-u ⸢TE⸣ [...] IQ BA x

(r 22) What did you ... [...] ..., who cut down this (tree)?

r 2323

GIŠu SIG₅u ⸢la⸣ te-⸢zi⸣-ba-a a-⸢na⸣ e-⸢le-na⸣-a-te

(r 23) Do not leave a fine tree to the witches!

r 2424

DUMU.MUNUS dḫum-bi ⸢kit?⸣-ti-i dan-nat gišTIR ere-⸢ni⸣

(r 24) O daughter of Humbaba, righteous (and) strong one of the cedar forest! They destroyed his ..., (and) her house, all [...].

r 2525

x-ka-šu₂ E₂-sa ⸢iḫ⸣-pu-u ⸢ka⸣-lu x [...] x

r 2626

[...] x-di-ra-a ⸢E? ḫa⸣-bi-ru ⸢ša?⸣ la-le-[e-a]

(r 26) ..., husband of [my] charms,

r 2727

[...]-še-ṣi ki-⸢i⸣ ina NAB RA x x [...]

(r 27) [...] ... as in ... [...]

r 2828

[...] x ⸢AD⸣-u-a ⸢GAZ⸣ pa-x [...]

(r 28) [...] ... my father is killed ... [...]

r 2929

[...]-ši u₂-se-⸢ṣu⸣-ni-i nu-[...]

(r 29) [...] ... they have released me ... [...].

Left
l.e. 1l.e. 1

[... lu₂]ENGAR KI lu₂*SIPA x [...] x MIR

(l.e. 1) [...] the farmer with the shepherd ... [...] ...

l.e. 22

1 9v

(l.e. 2) 1 9

l.e. 33

66

(l.e. 3) 66

l.e. 44

64

(l.e. 4) 64

1In Neo-Assyrian, long unstressed vowels are shortened. Hence, the plural verbal forms in this composition are normalized with a short final -u instead of a long one (cf. Hämeen-Anttila 2000: 29, 32-33).

1 Or : He has made.# I @?am undecided/powerless/anxious/hesitate as to its/his?@

2 In Neo-Assyrian texts , the šandabakku is usually "the governor of Nippur ". However , it is possible that the scribe of this composition confused šandabakku with šandanakku "the administrator of date orchards " which would make more sense in this context.

Created by Greta Van Buylaere for the AHRC-funded GKAB Project, 2011 and released under the Creative Commons Attribution Share-Alike license 3.0. Please cite this page as http://oracc.org/cams/gkab/P338675
